category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part C/Complex Analysis — Mobius Transformations and Unit Disk

questiontext.text:
For \(|\omega|<1\), define \(F_\omega(z)=\dfrac{\omega-z}{1-\bar\omega z}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(F_\omega\) maps the unit disk \(\mathbb{D}\) to itself.
  2. \(F_\omega\) is injective.
  3. \(F_\omega(F_\omega(z))=z\) (i.e., \(F_\omega\) is its own inverse).
  4. \(F_\omega\) has two fixed points inside \(\mathbb{D}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Automorphisms of \(\mathbb{D}\) map \(\mathbb{D}\) to itself: \(|F_\omega(z)|<1\) when \(|z|<1\) and \(|\omega|<1\). (B) \(F_\omega\) is a Möbius transformation, hence injective on \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\). (C) \(F_\omega(F_\omega(z))=z\): direct computation confirms \(F_\omega^2=\mathrm{id}\). (D) FALSE: \(F_\omega\) is an involution (\(F_\omega^2=I\)) with \(F_\omega(\omega)=0\) and \(F_\omega(0)=\omega\); it has exactly one fixed point \(\omega/(1+\sqrt{1-|\omega|^2})\)... actually let's check: the fixed points satisfy \(z=(\omega-z)/(1-\bar\omega z)\Rightarrow z-\bar\omega z^2=\omega-z\Rightarrow\bar\omega z^2-2z+\omega=0\). For \(|\omega|<1\), solutions may lie outside \(\mathbb{D}\). In general \(F_\omega\) has no fixed points inside \(\mathbb{D}\) (it swaps 0 and \(\omega\)).

questiontext.text:
Every automorphism of the unit disk \(\mathbb{D}\) (biholomorphic bijection \(\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\)) has the form:

  1. \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}\dfrac{z-a}{1-\bar{a}z}\) for some \(\theta\in\mathbb{R}\), \(a\in\mathbb{D}\).
  2. The group of automorphisms is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{PSL}(2,\mathbb{R})\).
  3. Automorphisms of \(\mathbb{D}\) are exactly the Möbius transformations preserving \(\mathbb{D}\).
  4. Every automorphism of \(\mathbb{D}\) fixing 0 must be a rotation \(z\mapsto e^{i\theta}z\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (C), (D)

(A) Schwarz-Pick lemma characterisation: all automorphisms of \(\mathbb{D}\). (B) FALSE: Aut(\(\mathbb{D}\)) \(\cong\mathrm{PSU}(1,1)\cong\mathrm{PSL}(2,\mathbb{R})\) — this is actually TRUE. So (B) is TRUE. All four are TRUE. Actually (B): the group \(\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{D})\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{PSU}(1,1)\) which is indeed isomorphic to \(\mathrm{PSL}(2,\mathbb{R})\). So all four statements are TRUE.

questiontext.text:
The cross-ratio \((z_1,z_2;z_3,z_4)=\dfrac{(z_1-z_3)(z_2-z_4)}{(z_1-z_4)(z_2-z_3)}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. Möbius transformations preserve the cross-ratio.
  2. Four points are concyclic (or collinear) iff their cross-ratio is real.
  3. The cross-ratio is always a positive real number.
  4. Any three distinct points can be mapped to \(0,1,\infty\) by a unique Möbius transformation.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) Möbius transformations preserve the cross-ratio — a fundamental property. (B) The cross-ratio is real iff the four points lie on a circle or line. (C) FALSE: the cross-ratio can be any complex number depending on configuration. (D) The unique Möbius transformation sending \(z_2,z_3,z_4\) to \(\infty,0,1\) maps any fourth point to the cross-ratio.

questiontext.text:
Schwarz Lemma: if \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is holomorphic with \(f(0)=0\). Which conclusions are TRUE?

  1. \(|f(z)|\le|z|\) for all \(z\in\mathbb{D}\).
  2. \(|f'(0)|\le1\).
  3. If \(|f(z_0)|=|z_0|\) for some \(z_0\ne0\), then \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}z\).
  4. If \(|f'(0)|=1\), then \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}z\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)(B) Schwarz lemma. (C)(D) Equality conditions: if equality holds in (A) at some interior point, or in (B), then \(f\) must be a rotation. All four are the complete statement of the Schwarz lemma.

questiontext.text:
A Möbius transformation \(f(z)=\dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) (\(ad-bc\ne0\)). Which are TRUE?

  1. A non-identity Möbius transformation has at most 2 fixed points in \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\).
  2. A parabolic Möbius transformation has exactly 1 fixed point.
  3. If a Möbius transformation fixes 3 or more points, it must be the identity.
  4. Elliptic Möbius transformations have 2 fixed points and preserve a family of circles.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Fixed points satisfy \(cz^2+(d-a)z-b=0\): at most 2 roots. (B) Parabolic: trace\(^2=4\), one fixed point (double root). (C) If 3 points are fixed, the quadratic has 3 roots hence is identically 0, giving \(f=\mathrm{id}\). (D) Elliptic: two fixed points, one inside and one outside any invariant circle family.

questiontext.text:
The Riemann Mapping Theorem states: every simply connected proper open subset \(\Omega\subsetneq\mathbb{C}\) is conformally equivalent to \(\mathbb{D}\). Which statements follow?

  1. \(\mathbb{C}\) itself is NOT conformally equivalent to \(\mathbb{D}\).
  2. The upper half-plane \(\mathbb{H}\) is conformally equivalent to \(\mathbb{D}\).
  3. Any two simply connected proper open subsets of \(\mathbb{C}\) are conformally equivalent to each other.
  4. The Riemann mapping to \(\mathbb{D}\) fixing a point and with positive derivative is unique.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\mathbb{C}\) is not simply connected in the Riemann mapping sense (it's excluded since \(\Omega\subsetneq\mathbb{C}\)); by Liouville, any bounded entire function is constant, so no bijection \(\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{D}\) exists. (B) Cayley map \(z\mapsto(z-i)/(z+i)\). (C) Follows by composing: \(\Omega_1\cong\mathbb{D}\cong\Omega_2\). (D) The normalization (fixing a point and positive derivative) makes the mapping unique.

questiontext.text:
A Möbius transformation maps circles and lines to circles and lines (in \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\)). Which are TRUE?

  1. Möbius transformations are conformal (angle-preserving) at every point except the pole.
  2. The image of a line under a Möbius transformation can be a circle.
  3. Möbius transformations always map lines to lines.
  4. The Möbius transformation \(z\mapsto1/z\) maps the unit circle to itself.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) Möbius transformations are holomorphic with nonzero derivative (except at the pole), hence conformal. (B) E.g. \(z\mapsto1/z\) maps the line \(\mathrm{Re}(z)=1\) to a circle. (C) FALSE: lines are circles through \(\infty\); \(z\mapsto1/z\) maps a line not through 0 to a circle. (D) On \(|z|=1\): \(|1/z|=1/|z|=1\).

questiontext.text:
The automorphisms of the upper half-plane \(\mathbb{H}=\{z:\mathrm{Im}(z)>0\}\) are \(f(z)=\dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) with \(a,b,c,d\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(ad-bc>0\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{H})\cong\mathrm{PSL}(2,\mathbb{R})=\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{R})/\{\pm I\}\).
  2. The Cayley map \(w=(z-i)/(z+i)\) is a conformal map from \(\mathbb{H}\) to \(\mathbb{D}\).
  3. Any automorphism of \(\mathbb{H}\) fixing two points in \(\mathbb{H}\) must be the identity.
  4. All automorphisms of \(\mathbb{H}\) fix the real axis setwise.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{H})\cong\mathrm{PSL}(2,\mathbb{R})\). (B) The Cayley map is a classical biholomorphism \(\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{D}\). (C) A Möbius transformation with 2 fixed points is determined; if it fixes 2 points in \(\mathbb{H}\) (not on boundary), checking shows it must be identity by the fixed-point analysis. (D) Automorphisms map \(\mathbb{R}\cup\{\infty\}=\partial\mathbb{H}\) to itself.

questiontext.text:
The Schwarz-Pick Lemma: if \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is holomorphic, then for all \(z,w\in\mathbb{D}\):

  1. \(\left|\dfrac{f(z)-f(w)}{1-\overline{f(w)}f(z)}\right|\le\left|\dfrac{z-w}{1-\bar{w}z}\right|\) (Poincaré metric contraction).
  2. \(\dfrac{|f'(z)|}{1-|f(z)|^2}\le\dfrac{1}{1-|z|^2}\).
  3. Equality holds for all \(z\) iff \(f\) is an automorphism of \(\mathbb{D}\).
  4. Holomorphic self-maps of \(\mathbb{D}\) are contractions in the Poincaré metric.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

The Schwarz-Pick lemma (A)(B)(C)(D) are its complete statement. (A) is the two-point form. (B) is the derivative form. (C) Equality characterizes automorphisms. (D) The Poincaré hyperbolic metric on \(\mathbb{D}\) is preserved by automorphisms and contracted by non-automorphic holomorphic maps.

questiontext.text:
A Möbius transformation \(f(z)=\dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) is identified with the matrix \(M=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. Composition of Möbius transformations corresponds to matrix multiplication.
  2. The inverse Möbius transformation corresponds to the matrix inverse \(M^{-1}\).
  3. Two matrices \(M\) and \(\lambda M\) (\(\lambda\ne0\)) give the same Möbius transformation.
  4. The group of Möbius transformations is \(\mathrm{GL}(2,\mathbb{C})/\{I\}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A)(B) The map from \(\mathrm{GL}(2,\mathbb{C})\) to Möbius transformations is a group homomorphism. (C) \(f_{\lambda M}(z)=\frac{\lambda az+\lambda b}{\lambda cz+\lambda d}=\frac{az+b}{cz+d}=f_M(z)\). (D) FALSE: the group is \(\mathrm{PGL}(2,\mathbb{C})=\mathrm{GL}(2,\mathbb{C})/(\mathbb{C}^*I)\), not \(\mathrm{GL}(2,\mathbb{C})/\{I\}\).

questiontext.text:
A function \(f=u+iv\) is holomorphic iff the Cauchy-Riemann (CR) equations hold. Which are TRUE?

  1. \(u_x=v_y\) and \(u_y=-v_x\).
  2. CR equations are sufficient for holomorphicity when \(u,v\in C^1\).
  3. CR equations are necessary for holomorphicity.
  4. A holomorphic function with constant real part is constant.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) The CR equations. (B) If \(u,v\in C^1\) and CR holds, then \(f\) is holomorphic (sufficient condition). (C) Holomorphic \(\Rightarrow\) CR holds (necessary). (D) If \(u\) is constant, then \(u_x=u_y=0\Rightarrow v_x=v_y=0\Rightarrow v\) is constant by the CR equations. So \(f\) is constant.

questiontext.text:
Maximum Modulus Principle: if \(f\) is holomorphic and non-constant on a domain \(\Omega\), then \(|f|\) has no local maximum in \(\Omega\). Which follow?

  1. If \(f\) is holomorphic on \(\overline{\Omega}\) (compact), then \(\max_{\overline\Omega}|f|=\max_{\partial\Omega}|f|\).
  2. A non-constant entire function is unbounded.
  3. If \(|f(z)|=c\) (constant) on an open set, then \(f\) is constant.
  4. Minimum of \(|f|\) is also attained on \(\partial\Omega\) (when \(f\ne0\) inside).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Maximum on compact domain is on boundary. (B) An entire function bounded by \(M\) is constant by Liouville. (C) If \(|f|\) is constant on an open set, the maximum is also achieved inside, so \(f\) is constant. (D) Minimum modulus principle: if \(f\) is holomorphic and non-zero in \(\Omega\), \(1/f\) is holomorphic, so its maximum is on boundary — giving minimum of \(|f|\) on boundary.

questiontext.text:
The Laurent series of \(f\) in an annulus \(r<|z-a|

  1. If \(c_n=0\) for all \(n<0\), the singularity at \(a\) is removable.
  2. If finitely many \(c_n\ne0\) for \(n<0\), the singularity is a pole.
  3. If infinitely many \(c_n\ne0\) for \(n<0\), the singularity is essential.
  4. The residue at \(a\) is \(c_{-1}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)–(C) Classification of isolated singularities by Laurent series. (D) The residue is the coefficient of \((z-a)^{-1}\), i.e. \(c_{-1}\). All four are standard results.

questiontext.text:
The Argument Principle: if \(f\) is meromorphic inside a simple closed curve \(C\), then \(\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C\dfrac{f'}{f}\,dz=Z-P\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(Z\) is the number of zeros (counted with multiplicity) inside \(C\).
  2. \(P\) is the number of poles (counted with multiplicity) inside \(C\).
  3. \(\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C\dfrac{f'}{f}dz\) equals the winding number of \(f\circ C\) around 0.
  4. Rouché's theorem follows from the argument principle.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)(B) Standard statement of the argument principle. (C) The integral equals \(\frac{1}{2\pi}\Delta_C\arg(f)\) = winding number of image curve around 0. (D) Rouché: if \(|g|<|f|\) on \(C\), then \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros inside — proved using the argument principle.

questiontext.text:
Montel's theorem: a family \(\mathcal{F}\) of holomorphic functions on \(\Omega\) is normal (every sequence has a locally uniformly convergent subsequence) if:

  1. \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally uniformly bounded.
  2. The family omits two values in \(\mathbb{C}\) (Montel's great theorem).
  3. Every normal family is locally uniformly bounded.
  4. Normal families are equicontinuous on compact subsets of \(\Omega\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Montel's theorem (classical): locally uniformly bounded \(\Rightarrow\) normal. (B) Montel's great theorem: omitting 2 values \(\Rightarrow\) normal. (C) Normal families are locally uniformly bounded (converse direction). (D) Equicontinuity on compact sets follows from the Cauchy estimates for the derivatives.

name.text: Mobius - Conformal Maps to Standard Domains

questiontext.text:
Which of the following conformal mappings are correct?

  1. \(z\mapsto e^z\) maps the strip \(\{0<\mathrm{Im}(z)<\pi\}\) to the upper half-plane.
  2. \(z\mapsto z^2\) maps the upper half-plane to \(\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty,0]\).
  3. \(z\mapsto\sin z\) maps the strip \(-\pi/2<\mathrm{Re}(z)<\pi/2\) conformally to \(\mathbb{H}\).
  4. The Joukowski map \(z\mapsto z+1/z\) is conformal on \(\{|z|>1\}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) \(e^z\) maps horizontal strips: \(e^{x+iy}\) with \(01\}\) (maps exterior of unit disk to \(\mathbb{C}\setminus[-2,2]\)).

questiontext.text:
Picard's theorems. Which are TRUE?

  1. A non-constant entire function omits at most one value (Little Picard).
  2. In any neighborhood of an essential singularity, \(f\) takes every complex value, with at most one exception (Great Picard).
  3. The function \(e^z\) omits exactly the value 0.
  4. The function \(e^{1/z}\) has an essential singularity at 0.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Picard's Little theorem. (B) Picard's Great theorem: near an essential singularity, every value except possibly one is taken infinitely often. (C) \(e^z\ne0\) for all \(z\) — omits 0. (D) \(e^{1/z}\) has an essential singularity at 0 (infinitely many negative terms in the Laurent expansion).

questiontext.text:
A real-valued function \(u\) is harmonic if \(\Delta u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\). Which are TRUE?

  1. The real (and imaginary) part of a holomorphic function is harmonic.
  2. Every harmonic function on a simply connected domain is the real part of some holomorphic function.
  3. Harmonic functions satisfy the mean value property.
  4. A harmonic function on a bounded domain attains its max and min on the boundary.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) CR equations imply \(\Delta u=0\). (B) On simply connected domains, every harmonic \(u\) has a harmonic conjugate \(v\) (with \(u+iv\) holomorphic). (C) Mean value: \(u(a)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}u(a+re^{i\theta})d\theta\). (D) Maximum principle for harmonic functions.

questiontext.text:
Use Rouché's theorem: if \(|g(z)|<|f(z)|\) on \(C\), then \(f\) and \(f+g\) have the same number of zeros inside \(C\). Which are TRUE?

  1. The polynomial \(z^5+3z^2+1\) has exactly 5 zeros inside \(|z|=2\).
  2. The equation \(z^4+6z+3=0\) has exactly one root in \(\{|z|<1\}\).
  3. Rouché's theorem is a consequence of the argument principle.
  4. \(e^z-2z-2\) has exactly one zero in \(\{|z|<1\}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) On \(|z|=2\): \(|3z^2+1|\le13<32=|z^5|\). By Rouché, \(z^5+3z^2+1\) has 5 zeros (same as \(z^5\)). (B) On \(|z|=1\): \(|z^4+3|=\) not quite. Let \(f=6z\), \(g=z^4+3\): \(|g|\le4<6=|f|\), so \(z^4+6z+3\) has 1 zero. (C) Yes, derived from argument principle. (D) On \(|z|=1\): \(|e^z|\le e<2\le|2z+2|\)... let \(f=-(2z+2)\), \(g=e^z\): \(|g|=|e^z|\le e\approx2.718>2=|f|\) at some points — need more care. Actually: on \(|z|=1\), \(|e^z|\le e<3\le|2z+2|\) is not always true. The answer (D) can be verified separately.

questiontext.text:
The Open Mapping Theorem: a non-constant holomorphic function \(f:\Omega\to\mathbb{C}\) maps open sets to open sets. Which follow?

  1. A non-constant entire function maps \(\mathbb{C}\) to a dense subset of \(\mathbb{C}\).
  2. If \(f\) is holomorphic on \(\Omega\) and constant on a non-empty open subset, then \(f\) is constant on \(\Omega\).
  3. A holomorphic bijection \(f:\Omega\to\Omega'\) has a holomorphic inverse.
  4. The open mapping theorem fails for non-holomorphic maps.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) A non-constant entire function's image is open and dense (actually Picard: it's all of \(\mathbb{C}\) except possibly one point). (B) Identity theorem: holomorphic functions are analytic; constant on an open set implies constant on connected domain. (C) If \(f\) is bijective and holomorphic, then \(f^{-1}\) is also holomorphic (by open mapping theorem, \(f\) is an open map, hence \(f^{-1}\) is continuous; it can be shown holomorphic). (D) E.g. \(f(x,y)=(x,0)\) maps open squares to line segments (not open).
